calendar.matters:
Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn For Preliminary Injunctive Relief Or For Stay
Off Calendar Jul-23-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Anna Mar, 415-425-8223 Petitioner in Pro Per.
Peter Miljanich, 415-554-4700, Deputy City Attorney of the Office of the City Attorney for Respondent.
Michael Dorsi, Esq. 415-795-3579 of Ad Astra for Real Party Brandon Rasmusson.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court shares its views that neither factor re: prevailing on the merits or a showing of irreparable harm have been satisifed. Petitioner argues. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

PETITIONER ANNA MAR's Motion For Preliminary Injunctive Relief Or For Stay.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

Petitioner Anna Mar's motion for preliminary injunction or stay is denied. Petitioner does not demonstrate a likelihood of prevailing on the merits or irreparable harm.

Petitioner fails to show that Respondent's refusal to grant discretionary review of the Rasmusson's building permit was an abuse of discretion. Respondent has broad discretion in determining whether to grant discretionary review. (See Guinnane v. San Francisco City Planning Com., (1989) 209 Cal.App.3d 732, 740; Ideal Boat & Camper Storage v. City of Alameda (2012) 208 Cal.App.4th 301, 317.) Respondent's guidelines provide that discretionary review is appropriate in "exceptional and extraordinary circumstances." (Petitioner's Ex Parte Application, Ex. 6-3, pg. 2.) Such circumstances do not exist in this case. The overwhelming evidence in the record, i.e., the Shea Report, establishes that the Rasmusson's renovations will not meaningfully harm Petitioner's tree.

Respondent did not abuse its discretion or neglect to perform a mandatory duty. Public Resources Code 21000 and San Francisco Planning Code 101 make broad pronouncements about protecting the general welfare and the environment. Such amorphous provisions do not clearly define the specific duties or course of conduct that respondent must take and therefore, they are not the proper prerequisite for a traditional mandamus action. (See Schwartz v. Poizner (2010) 187 Cal.App.4th 592, 596-597 [describing ministerial act]; Morris v. Harper (2001) 94 Cal.App.4th 52, 58 ["mandate will lie to compel performance of a clear, present, and usually ministerial duty"]; Bullis Charter School v. Los Altos School Dist. (2011) 200 Cal.App.4th 1022, 1035 [mandamus applies where the statute "clearly defines the specific duties or course of conduct"].)

Read together, Respondent's Discretionary Review Action DRA-0598, Discretionary Review Abbreviated Analysis, and the Board of Appeal's Final Decision, make it clear that Petitioner's appeal was denied because the permit was properly issued and there were no extraordinary circumstances to warrant discretionary review. Respondent's decision complies with Topanga Ass'n for a Scenic Community v. City & County of Los Angeles (1974) 11 Cal.3d 506, 515. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Anthony Vaughn, CSR#6185, tonyvaughn@yahoo.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
